cue: How do you say someone is in a foul mood?

es.literal: to have one's guitar badly tuned
es.meaning: To be in a bad mood; to be out of sorts.
es.example: Hoy el jefe tiene mal templada la guitarra.
es.exampleTranslation: The boss is in a mood today.
es.regionNote: Reviewer to confirm MX currency; 'estar de malas' is the everyday alternative.

it.meaning: To be in a bad mood; to be easily irritated.
it.example: Oggi il capo ha la luna storta.
it.exampleTranslation: The boss is in a mood today.

bridge.note: A bad mood explained by two different systems. Spanish blames the INSTRUMENT: the guitar is out of tune, so everything the person produces comes out sour — a fault in the works, and fixable. Italian blames the SKY: the moon is crooked today, a condition imposed from outside, nobody's fault and nothing to be done until it passes. One diagnosis suggests tuning; the other suggests waiting.

interference.es_to_it: Guitars mean nothing to an Italian mood; the moon governs it — avere la luna storta (or essere di luna storta).
interference.it_to_es: 'Tener la luna torcida' is not Spanish; the badly tuned guitar carries it.

review.notes: ES side discovered in the Spanish idioms book Joe is reading, 2026-07-21 (discovery only; all glosses and examples authored fresh). IT counterpart from Claude's recall — UNVERIFIED. Fixable-vs-fated framing. The ES side may be regional — flagged.
